data.title: Private-Equity Backed Insurer CFC Weighs London Listing, Targeting A Potential 5 Billion Stg Valuation - FT
data.description:
• PRIVATE-EQUITY BACKED INSURER CFC WEIGHS LONDON LISTING - FT

  • CYBER SPECIALIST GROUP CFC, OWNED BY EQT AND VITRUVIAN IS TARGETING A POTENTIAL £5BN VALUATION - FT

data.title: CITIC Securities Company Unit Issues $605 Million Bonds
data.description:
CITIC Securities' indirect subsidiary, CSI MTN, issued $605.2 million in medium-term bonds to supplement working capital.

The notes are guaranteed by subsidiary CITIC Securities International, according to a Friday filing with the Shanghai bourse. Total guaranteed notes have risen to $2.88 billion.

data.title: Malaysia Import Growth at 3-Month Low
data.description:
Imports to Malaysia edged up 1.2% year-on-year to MYR 113.13 billion in June 2025, missing market estimates of 9.7% and sharply easing from a 6.6% growth in the previous month.

It was the weakest rise in purchases since March, dragged by the spillover effects of sweeping U.S. tariffs.

data.title: Eiffage to Build Frontex's New HQ in Poland
data.description:
Eiffage said Thursday it won a contract to design and build the European Border and Coast Guard Agency Frontex's new headquarters in Warsaw, Poland.

The 69,000-square-meter building in the Mokotów district will have seven floors with two basement levels. Construction is slated to commence in January 2026 with completion expected in 2028.

data.title: Hillgrove Resources Says June Quarter Copper Production Falls; Shares Jump 8%
data.description:
Hillgrove Resources said that its copper production for the June quarter from the Kanmantoo project in South Australia came in at 2,593 tonnes, down more than 12%, resulting in a C1 operating cost of AU$5.24 per pound sold, according to a Friday Australian bourse filing.

Total inventory mined remained broadly flat at 336,000 tonnes, and the average mined grade declined to 0.8%.

Chief Executive Bob Fulker said that an issue with two of the mining areas delayed access to higher-grade copper ore during the period, and its total copper production is now tracking toward the lower end of its 2025 guidance range of 12,000 to 14,000 tonnes.

Mine development increased nearly 11% quarter-over-quarter to 2,011 meters.

Hillgrove Resources' shares advanced 8% in recent trading on Friday.

data.title: Trump Nears Victory on Clawing Back Foreign Aid, Public Media Funding — WSJ
data.description:
By Ken Thomas, Lindsay Wise and Jasmine Li

WASHINGTON — Congress closed in on canceling $9 billion in federal spending for foreign aid and public broadcasting, following through on President Trump's efforts to defund the programs and overcoming some resistance among Republican lawmakers.

The Senate passed the White House-endorsed plan early Thursday morning after the GOP-led chamber blocked attempts to slim down the package. The measure was approved 51-48 at 2:30 a.m., largely along party lines, with Republican Sens. Susan Collins of Maine and Lisa Murkowski of Alaska joining Democrats in opposition.

The House was expected to clear the measure as soon as Thursday evening, acting before a Friday deadline after which the executive branch is supposed to release the funding if Congress doesn't act. The final vote was delayed as Democrats tried to introduce a bipartisan amendment that would require Attorney General Pam Bondi to release records related to convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein within 30 days. Republicans offered their own resolution to release the files, but Democrats complained that the GOP measure wasn't binding.

The anticipated passage of the rescissions package would again show off Trump's ability to muscle legislation through the narrow GOP majorities in Congress and pressure holdout Republican lawmakers to get in line. The cuts are set to be the first time a White House has accomplished clawbacks in more than a quarter-century, and White House officials made clear that they would pursue additional cuts.

"Congress wants to be a part of voting for these cuts and making them permanent," said Russ Vought, the director of the White House Office of Management and Budget. Vought, speaking at a Christian Science Monitor breakfast, said the Trump administration was likely to send up another proposal to recover more funding, but he declined to provide specific details.

Senate Majority Leader John Thune (R., S.D.) said the Senate was doing its part to cut waste, calling it a "small but important step toward fiscal sanity." The $9 billion in cuts represent about one-10th of 1% of the roughly $7 trillion federal budget. Most of the federal government's spending is on Social Security, Medicare and related health programs, as well as interest on debt.

The rescission votes came just weeks after Congress passed Trump's "one, big beautiful bill," which extended tax cuts and introduced new ones while cutting spending on Medicaid and food aid. The law will increase budget deficits by an estimated $3.4 trillion through 2034, compared with doing nothing, according to the Congressional Budget Office.

"They are pretending to be fiscal hawks," Sen. Patty Murray (D., Wash.) said of Republicans.

Some Republicans who had raised concerns about the rescissions package ended up voting for it Thursday.

"I suspect we're going to find out there are some things that we're going to regret...And I suspect when we do, we'll have to come back and fix them," Sen. Thom Tillis (R., N.C.) said in a speech explaining his support.

The congressional action effectively codifies steps by the administration to slash foreign-aid programs, including the United States Agency for International Development, which was shuttered earlier this year and folded into the State Department. It also struck a major blow to the Corporation for Public Broadcasting, a private, nonprofit corporation that was authorized during the administration of President Lyndon B. Johnson in 1967.

The bill proposes that lawmakers claw back funding they previously doled out, including $7.9 billion from foreign-aid programs. The plan also would rescind $1.1 billion from the CPB — the organization that oversees government funding for National Public Radio and the Public Broadcasting Service — defunding it for the next two fiscal years.

Republicans have long criticized NPR and PBS for what they see as a liberal bias. Trump last week threatened Republican senators that he would withhold his endorsement from anyone who opposed the cuts.

"The government doesn't have any business getting into the media business," said Sen. John Cornyn (R., Texas).

While NPR and PBS and their local stations also receive funds through donations from viewers as well as foundations, that won't make up for the shortfall from the loss of federal money. The CPB has said more than 70% of its annual appropriations go to the more than 1,500 public radio and television stations.

Stations outside of major markets such as New York and Los Angeles will be particularly hard hit. The CPB is expected to seek funding during the next regular appropriations process, people familiar with the matter said.

"This elimination of federal funding will decimate public media and put local stations at risk of going dark, cutting off service to communities that rely on them," said Kate Riley, president and CEO of America's Public Television Stations, which lobbies on behalf of noncommercial stations.

Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer (D., N.Y.) and other Democrats complained that Republicans were ceding Congress's power of the purse to the executive branch. The GOP was turning the Senate "into a subservient rubber stamp for the executive, at the behest of Donald Trump," Schumer said.

Vought, in the breakfast appearance, predicted a tougher stance on the congressional appropriations process, which determines where money is spent, telling reporters that it should rely on less cooperation between the two parties. "Who ran and won on an agenda of a bipartisan appropriations process? Literally, no one," he said, adding that "the appropriations process has to be less bipartisan."

Murkowski, a senior Republican on the Senate Appropriations Committee, said Vought "thinks that we are irrelevant." She said the rescissions process, which requires a majority vote rather than the 60 votes typically required in the Senate, wasn't the right way to cut spending.

"It's not legislating, it's basically the White House saying, 'this is what we want you to do. Take it or leave it. Trust us,'" Murkowski said. Republican senators did reach an agreement on Tuesday to save $400 million of funding for the Pepfar HIV/AIDS relief program. Funding for maternal health, malaria and tuberculosis also will be explicitly protected by new language added, people familiar with the matter said. Sen. Mike Rounds (R., S.D.) said he had reached an agreement with the White House to redirect $9.4 million in funds to support 28 Native American radio stations in nine states, including four in his home state.

Write to Ken Thomas at ken.thomas@wsj.com, Lindsay Wise at lindsay.wise@wsj.com and Jasmine Li at jasmine.li@wsj.com

data.title: Malaysia Trade Surplus Narrows in June
data.description:
Malaysia’s trade surplus narrowed sharply to MYR 8.6 billion in June 2025, down from MYR 14.3 billion in the same month last year and well below market expectations of MYR 10.4 billion, as exports declined while imports rose.

Exports unexpectedly fell by 3.5% year-on-year to a four-month low of MYR 121.7 billion—the second consecutive monthly decline, following an upwardly revised 1.1% drop in May and missing forecasts of a 5.6% increase.

Meanwhile, imports rose by 1.2% to MYR 113.1 billion, significantly below expectations for a 9.7% rise.

data.title: XRP Price Skyrockets—Is a $4 Target Now Within Reach?
data.description:
XRP price started a fresh increase and surged above the $3.350 zone. The price is now consolidating gains and might continue to rise above the $3.60 zone.

  • XRP price started a fresh increase above the $3.350 zone.
  • The price is now trading above $3.40 and the 100-hourly Simple Moving Average.
  • There is a key bullish trend line forming with support at $3.450 on the hourly chart of the XRP/USD pair (data source from Kraken).
  • The pair could start another increase if it stays above the $3.220 zone.

XRP Price Rallies Over 15%

XRP price started a fresh increase after it settled above the $3.00 level, beating Bitcoin and Ethereum. The price was able to climb above the $3.220 resistance level.

The bulls remained in action and the price gained pace for a move above $3.350 barrier. Finally, the price tested the $3.650 zone. A high was formed at $3.660 and the price is now consolidating gains. There was a move below the $3.60 level but stayed above the 23.6% Fib retracement level of the upward move from the $2.80 swing low to the $3.660 high.

The price is now trading above $3.50 and the 100-hourly Simple Moving Average. There is also a key bullish trend line forming with support at $3.450 on the hourly chart of the XRP/USD pair.

On the upside, the price might face resistance near the $3.620 level. The first major resistance is near the $3.660 level. A clear move above the $3.660 resistance might send the price toward the $3.750 resistance. Any more gains might send the price toward the $3.80 resistance or even $3.880 in the near term. The next major hurdle for the bulls might be near the $4.00 zone.

Downside Break?

If XRP fails to clear the $3.660 resistance zone, it could start another decline. Initial support on the downside is near the $3.450 level and the trend line zone. The next major support is near the $3.350 level.

If there is a downside break and a close below the $3.350 level, the price might continue to decline toward the $3.320 support. The next major support sits near the $3.220 zone.

Technical Indicators

Hourly MACD – The MACD for XRP/USD is now gaining pace in the bullish zone.

Hourly RSI (Relative Strength Index) – The RSI for XRP/USD is now above the 50 level.

Major Support Levels – $3.450 and $3.350.

Major Resistance Levels – $3.660 and $3.80.

data.title: State Grid Information & Communication's Three Subsidiaries Wins Chinese State Grid's Bid for 966 Million Yuan
data.description:
State Grid Information & Communication's three subsidiaries won the State Grid Corporation of China's procurement bids for substation equipment for 966 million yuan, according to a Shanghai bourse filing on Friday.

Anhui Jiyuan Software, Beijing Zhongdian Feihua Communication, and State Grid ICT Yili Technology were the winners for the bid for equipment, which included cables, the state-owned information communication equipment producer said.

The company's shares rose over 1% during late morning trade.

data.title: CWT International Signs Copper Supply Deal With HNA Technology Unit; HNA Shares Jump 10%
data.description:
CWT International wholly owned Swiss subsidiary MRI Trading has entered into a commodity sales agreement with GTS Shipping, an indirect wholly owned unit of HNA Technology , according to a Thursday Hong Kong bourse filing.

Under the agreement, MRI Trading will supply copper cathodes worth up to $5 million.

Shares of CWT were down about 4% in Friday's recent trade., while HNA Technology Stock jumped nearly 10%.

The deal, signed July 17, covers the delivery of 200 to 500 metric tons of non-LME grade A copper from the Democratic Republic of Congo to be shipped to Shanghai.

CWT said the deal supports its commodity marketing business and will contribute to gross profit growth.

data.title: Insurer CFC weighs over $6.5 billion London listing, FT reports
data.description:
European private-equity firms EQT and Vitruvian Partners-backed specialist insurance provider CFC is considering a UK listing that could value the firm at more than 5 billion pounds ($6.71 billion), the Financial Times reported on Friday.

($1 = 0.7448 pounds)

data.title: India's Afcons Infrastructure gains after winning Croatia motorway contracts
data.description:
** Shares of Afcons Infrastructure rise as much as 5.6% to 437 rupees, logging biggest intraday pct gain since May 13

data.title: Enterprise Development Unit Exits US Tech Holdings; Shares Gain 2%
data.description:
Enterprise Development said its wholly owned unit sold shares in four US-listed securities between February and July, generating total proceeds of about $21.7 million, according to a Thursday Hong Kong bourse filing.

The disposals led to an estimated gain of $2.8 million.

Shares of the company were up about 2% in Friday's recent trade.

The transactions involved 37,002 shares in AMD, 15,000 in Meta Platforms, 48,000 ProShares UltraPro QQQ units, and 47,017 Nvidia shares.

It no longer holds any of these securities.

The sales were made to restructure its investment portfolio and free up funds for general working capital and repayment of a margin loan, according to the filing.

data.title: Bitcoin Trades Above $117K as Whale Deposits Decline and Stablecoin Inflows Rise
data.description:
Bitcoin continues to maintain upward momentum despite a recent pullback from its all-time high. Currently trading at $117,847, the asset has recorded nearly a 10% gain over the past week.

The dip from peak levels, approximately a 4.1% decline, has not dampened broader investor sentiment, with several on-chain indicators suggesting renewed buying interest and reduced selling pressure.

Bitcoin Whale Withdrawals Decline, While Stablecoins Flow In

In a recent analysis posted to CryptoQuant’s QuickTake platform, analyst Amr Taha shared insights pointing to a strategic change in behavior among key Bitcoin holders and investors.

The report, titled “Stablecoin Flood and Whale Retreat: Binance Moves Foreshadow Risk-On Sentiment”, outlined significant trends in whale activity and stablecoin flows that may support continued bullish momentum in the near term.

Taha’s research highlighted a steep reduction in whale-level Bitcoin deposits on Binance. Over the past 30 days, these deposits have dropped from $6.75 billion to $4.5 billion, a $2.25 billion decline.

Historically, large deposits from whales to centralized exchanges often signal an intention to sell, so the recent drop may imply a reduction in immediate sell-side pressure. This could stabilize Bitcoin’s price in the short term, especially if whales continue to hold or move assets to cold storage instead of preparing them for sale.

At the same time, stablecoin flows have increased dramatically across major exchanges. On July 16, Binance and HTX saw combined stablecoin inflows exceeding $1.7 billion.

Taha interpreted this as an indication that large entities, possibly institutions or whales, are preparing to accumulate digital assets. Large stablecoin deposits often precede significant buying activity, suggesting that the market could be gearing up for another leg higher, particularly if paired with reduced sell-side movements.

Macroeconomic Developments and Miner Sentiment Add Context

This on-chain activity is unfolding amid broader economic and political developments. Taha’s report also pointed to speculation around President Donald Trump’s comments during a private meeting, in which he reportedly considered replacing Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell.

Though later denied, the remark sparked reactions in traditional markets, including a weaker dollar and rising bond yields. These shifts signaled a rotation into risk assets, potentially benefiting crypto markets as investors reallocate capital in anticipation of a more accommodative monetary stance.

Separately, CryptoQuant analyst Arab Chain analyzed Bitcoin’s miner profitability using the Puell Multiple indicator. The data shows that while miners are currently making solid profits, the level has not reached historical peaks seen during prior market tops.

In the 2017 and 2021 cycles, extreme miner profitability (indicated by Puell readings exceeding 2.0–3.0) often preceded sharp price corrections. At current levels, Arab Chain believes the market is not in a euphoric state, reducing the likelihood of imminent volatility due to miner-driven selloffs.

Featured image created with DALL-E, Chart from TradingView

data.title: QIP fundraising hits five-year peak in July so far, SBI leads with landmark Rs 25,000 crore issue
data.description:
Qualified Institutional Placements (QIPs) saw a robust resurgence in July so far, with total fundraising touching a five-year high of over Rs 30,470 crore across ten issuances. This marks the strongest monthly performance since September 2020, when six companies collectively raised more than Rs 39,000 crore through QIPs.

Market analysts attribute this momentum to listed companies effectively tapping into institutional liquidity. Despite moderate returns, institutional investors continue to show strong appetite for such offerings.

According to Ajay Garg, CEO of SMC Global Securities, investors are increasingly favouring QIPs and IPOs over secondary market purchases due to more attractive valuations, access to quality stocks at issue prices, and strong post-listing performance. He added that QIPs offer companies a faster and more cost-efficient route to raise growth capital or reduce debt, further aided by regulatory clarity and improved governance — signalling deeper market maturity and expanding investor participation.

A major highlight in July was the State Bank of India’s record-breaking QIP on July 17, which raised nearly Rs 25,000 crore — the largest-ever QIP by a single entity. Investor interest was notably strong, with demand reportedly exceeding the supply by nearly three times.

Other significant QIP transactions during the month included CG Power raising over Rs 3,000 crore, Marathon Nextgen collecting Rs 900 crore, and Navin Fluorine securing Rs 750 crore. Additional participants in July's QIP spree included Phantom Digital, Shakti Pumps, Rajoo Engineers, and Tinna Rubber, among others.

Beyond July, 2025 has already witnessed several large-scale QIPs. Biocon raised Rs 4,500 crore, Hitachi Energy mobilised Rs 2,500 crore, IREDA brought in Rs 2,000 crore, while both UCO Bank and Capri Global Capital secured Rs 2,000 crore each. In June alone, seven companies raised over Rs 14,084 crore.

So far in 2025, a total of 30 companies have raised nearly Rs 60,000 crore via QIPs, building on the strong momentum from 2024, when 95 firms collectively raised more than Rs 1.37 lakh crore.Commenting on the trend, independent analyst Ajay Bagga highlighted that falling domestic interest rates, ample local liquidity, rising equity culture, inflows from domestic as well as foreign, and the ease of QIP allotments are all contributing to this surge. While there are concerns around promoter stake dilution, the market appears focused on long-term growth potential, choosing to look past short-term issues.

data.title: EMEA Morning Briefing: Upbeat Corporate Earnings Boost Optimism
data.description:
MARKET WRAPS

Watch For:

No major economic events expected; trading updates from Telenor, SKF, Burberry, Telia, Danske Bank

Opening Call:

European stock futures traded higher early Friday. Asian stock benchmarks were mostly higher; the dollar inched lower; Treasury yields were down; while oil futures and gold weakened.

Equities:

Stock futures were up, as investors continue to look to corporate earnings for cues. Upbeat earnings from some major U.S. companies and strong U.S. retail sales data have lifted optimism that markets can weather tariffs and shifting trade policy.

"Relative to expectations around Liberation Day, tariffs may have a more idiosyncratic impact, depending on the country and sector, given their piecemeal application, delayed starts and potential deals," said Morgan Stanley investment strategist Monica Guerra.

Forex:

The U.S. dollar shuffled between gains and losses amid mixed signals. On the one hand, stronger-than-expected U.S. economic data released overnight could dent rate-cut expectations, thereby supporting the greenback.

However, "USD remains vulnerable to the downside if concerns about U.S. policymaking further undermine investor confidence in USD assets," CBA's Carol Kong said. The dollar's weakness earlier this week due to worries over Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell's possible dismissal by President Trump is a case in point, she said.

Bonds:

Treasury yields are holding at levels near key numbers, including 4.5% for the 10-year, and 5% for the 30-year, suggesting that tariffs are only having a mild impact on asset prices, strategists said. But that could change if inflation sets in more durably.

"The Fed will try to see this as transitory," said John Briggs, head of U.S. rates strategy at Natixis Corporate and Investment Banking.

Fed Gov. Christopher Waller called for the central bank to cut interest rates at its July meeting, breaking ranks with Powell. "The risks to the economy are weighted toward cutting sooner rather than later," Waller said, as President Trump intensifies pressure on the Fed to lower borrowing costs.

Energy:

Oil futures consolidated amid mixed signals. On the demand side, economic resilience in the U.S. and China has provided some support, Tickmill's Joseph Dahrieh said.

However, this has been tempered by large increases in U.S. gasoline and diesel stockpiles, casting doubt on the strength of fuel demand as the peak summer travel season progresses, he said.

Metals:

Gold edged higher on possible position adjustments amid a lack of fresh catalysts. However, price gains may be curbed by overnight U.S. data, which showed economic resilience that could damp Fed rate-cut expectations.

-

Iron ore strengthened, buoyed by investors' optimism about improving steel mill margins amid China's efforts to remove overcapacity, and prospects for fresh stimulus for the property sector, ANZ Research said.

The policy that will have the best chance of stabilizing the Chinese property sector is the government buying up excess housing inventory, which can improve the developers' credit health and ease their financial stress, CBA's Vivek Dhar said.

TODAY'S TOP HEADLINES

The Global Risks That Come With the Loss of an Independent Fed

President Trump's threat to attempt to fire Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell has raised a pressing but potentially unanswerable question: What would the global economy and financial markets look like without an independent U.S. central bank?

Shielded from White House interference, the independent Fed has increasingly served as an anchor for U.S. and global markets, stepping in to steady the ship during the 2008-09 financial crisis, the Covid pandemic and other shocks of recent years. Economists credit the central bank's ability to help keep things stable in large part to its power to make moves it deems necessary, regardless of politics.

Bonds Breathe a Sign of Relief at Mild Tariff Data. What Could Change That.

A whiff of tariff inflation showed up in economic data this week, providing the first clues of how tariff policy might impact prices and the economy.

Signals from the bond market suggest tariffs are only having a mild impact on asset prices, strategists say. But that could change if inflation sets in more durably.

German Startup Wants to Regrow Europe's 'Spine' With AI Fighter Pilots, Drone Walls

MUNICH-Inside an unassuming redbrick building tucked beside railroad tracks in the Bavarian capital, engineers behind doors marked "maximum security" toil on drone prototypes, flight simulators and lines of code they believe will shape the next era of warfare.

Helsing, a 4-year-old artificial-intelligence defense startup, has quietly emerged as one of Europe's fastest-growing companies-and a poster child for a continent urgently re-engineering its defense in an era of fracturing alliances.

BHP Flags Higher Costs, Delays on Potash Project; May Sell Nickel Arm

Mining giant BHP Group flagged rising costs and delays at a giant potash project in Canada and said it may sell its Australian nickel operations, as it reported record annual iron ore and copper production.

Australia-based BHP, the world's biggest miner by market value, has been reshaping its business to bet on an accelerating energy transition and global population growth via investments in copper and potash, a fertilizer ingredient. The miner currently relies on the steel ingredient iron ore for more than half its earnings.

Netflix Raises Revenue, Margin Forecasts After Strong Second Quarter

Netflix raised its revenue and operating margin forecasts for the year, continuing a strong run that has propelled its shares to new highs.

The streaming service said member additions, price increases and its growing ad business drove revenue in the second quarter.

Boeing to Sell Up to 18 Dreamliner Jets to Gulf Air

Boeing plans to sell up to 18 of its 787 Dreamliner jets to Gulf Air.

The jet maker said Thursday it has agreed to sell 12 of the jets, with options for six more.

Write to singaporeeditors@dowjones.com

Expected Major Events for Friday

06:00/GER: Jun PPI

07:00/SPN: May Trade Balance

07:30/POR: May Balance of Payments

08:00/EU: May Euro area balance of payments

08:30/UK: Jun Monthly Insolvency statistics

09:00/EU: May Construction output

09:00/ITA: May Balance of Payments

All times in GMT. Powered by Onclusive and Dow Jones.

Write to us at newsletters@dowjones.com

We offer an enhanced version of this briefing that is optimized for viewing on mobile devices and sent directly to your email inbox. If you would like to sign up, please go to https://newsplus.wsj.com/subscriptions.

This article is a text version of a Wall Street Journal newsletter published earlier today.

data.title: Yangzhou Tinfulong to Offer About 40 Million Shares in Shanghai IPO
data.description:
Yangzhou Tinfulong (SHA:603406) is looking to offer up to 40,010,000 shares in its initial public offering on the Shanghai bourse.

The fiber materials producer's shares on offer represent 10% of the entire share capital following the offering, according to a Friday filing with the Shanghai Stock Exchange.

Issuance is expected on July 28.

CITIC Construction Investment Securities is the sponsor and lead underwriter of the offering.

data.title: Bora Pharmaceuticals Cuts Stock Dividend Rate
data.description:
Bora Pharmaceuticals lowered its stock dividend rate from 2 shares per share to about 1.9979 shares per share, or 199.7915 shares per 1,000 shares, according to a Thursday Taiwan Exchange filing.

Shares gained over 1% in late-morning trade Friday.

The total payout, however, remained unchanged at NT$206.8 million.

The revision follows an increase in outstanding shares due to employee stock warrants and convertible bond conversions, it said.

data.title: Soybeans head for 2.5% weekly gain on US demand hopes
data.description:
Chicago soybean futures rose on Friday, on track for a weekly gain of about 2.5%, supported by hopes for improved U.S. exports and expectations that the country's biofuel policy would boost demand for soyoil used as feedstock.

Despite the prices being less than $1 a bushel above last year's five-year lows, the plentiful supply from South America and projections of a large U.S. harvest capped further gains.

Corn futures also rose and were set to end the week up around 3% due to a wave of bargain-hunting and short-covering, though supply remains ample.

Wheat climbed but was headed for a weekly loss of about 1.5% amid seasonal pressure from ongoing northern hemisphere harvests.

The most active soybean contract on the Chicago Board of Trade (CBOT) was up 0.6% at $10.32-1/2 a bushel by 0331 GMT.

A weaker dollar helped propel gains by making U.S. farm goods cheaper for overseas buyers.

CBOT soyoil (BOc1) rose 0.5% and was headed for a roughly 4% weekly gain after touching its highest level in nearly two years.

U.S. policies restricting the range of non-soy feedstocks that can be used to make biodiesel lifted soyoil but beans will struggle to sustain a rally, said Tobin Gorey, founder of commodities consultancy Cornucopia.

"Soybean supply is neutral," he said. "There's not a lot of traction for prices."

U.S. soybean export sales in the week ended July 10 reached 529,600 metric tons for 2025-26 shipment, the U.S. Department of Agriculture (USDA) said, beating analysts' expectations.

The USDA also this week reported a sale of 120,000 tons of U.S. soybeans to "unknown destinations", triggering speculation that China might be the buyer and could buy again.

A trade deal between the United States and Indonesia could boost U.S. soy exports.

However, oilseed lobby group Abiove raised its forecast for Brazil's 2024/25 soybean exports and the Rosario Grains Exchange lifted its estimate for Argentina's 2024/25 harvest.

In other crops, CBOT corn was up 0.9% at $4.24-3/4 a bushel and wheat was 0.5% higher at $5.36 a bushel.

data.title: ArcelorMittal to Supply Reinforcing Steel for Railway Project in Germany
data.description:
ArcelorMittal will supply carbon dioxide-reduced reinforcing steel for the development of the U5 underground railway line in Hamburg, Germany.

The steelmaker said Thursday it will deliver its XCarb recycled and renewably produced steel for the Hamburger U5 project.

data.title: KOSPI Falls as Financials Weigh
data.description:
The benchmark KOSPI fell 0.55% to around 3,174 on Friday, giving back gains from the previous session, as losses in financial stocks and ongoing trade-related caution weighed on the index.

Market anxiety persisted amid uncertainty over South Korea’s efforts to secure favorable terms ahead of the looming US tariff deadline.

In the corporate sector, financials dropped nearly 2%, led by sharp losses in Shinhan Financial Group (-2.15%), KB Financial Group (-1.39%), and Hana Financial Group (-0.76%).

Other sectors also faced pressure, including consumer non-durables, industrial services, and transportation, such as KT&G Corporation (-0.92%), Samsung C&T Corporation (-5.53%), and Hyundai Merc Mar (-1.78%).

On the corporate front, South Korea’s EV exports jumped 11.2% year-on-year to $780 million in June, marking the first annual increase in 16 months.

Over the week, the benchmark index is poised for little change after a 4% gain last week.

data.title: Sweden's Husqvarna Names New CEO
data.description:
Husqvarna (HUSQ-A.ST, HUSQ-B.ST) said Thursday it named Glen Instone as its new chief executive officer, effective Aug. 11.

Instone, currently the president of the Husqvarna Forest and Garden Division, will succeed Pavel Hajman, who will leave the Swedish forest and gardening power tools manufacturing company by the end of 2025.

data.title: Wipro Clocks Gains in Fiscal Q1 Consolidated Profit
data.description:
Wipro recorded a rise in its consolidated attributable profit to 33.3 billion Indian rupees in the fiscal first quarter ended June 30, against 30.0 billion rupees a year ago.

Earnings per share grew to 3.17 rupees compared with the year-ago figure of 2.87 rupees, according to a post-market hours filing on Thursday filing by the IT services major. The EPS was higher than the 3.08 rupees estimated by the analysts polled by Visible Alpha

Revenue from operations in fiscal Q1 also increased to 221.35 billion rupees from 219.64 billion rupees a year earlier. This was lower than Visible Alpha's estimate of 220.6 billion rupees.

The company declared an interim dividend of 5 rupees per equity share of 2 rupees each, to be paid by Aug. 15.

data.title: US 10-Year Bond Yield Drops
data.description:
The yield on the 10-year US Treasury note dropped to 4.43% on Friday, even as strong economic data released overnight gave the Federal Reserve cover to delay cutting interest rates.

Retail sales rose more than expected in June, while weekly initial jobless claims unexpectedly dropped to a three-month low—adding to a string of robust economic data that underscore the resilience of the US economy.

Meanwhile, Trump on Wednesday clarified that he is not planning to fire Fed Chair Jerome Powell, but he kept the door open to the possibility.

Trump has repeatedly criticized Powell for not slashing interest rates more quickly, fueling speculation that his potential nominee for Fed Chair would push the FOMC toward a more dovish stance.

This has raised concerns over long-term inflation risks and the possibility that the Fed may lose control over yields on longer-dated maturities.

data.title: Malaysia's economy grew 4.5% y/y in Q2, advance estimates show
data.description:
Malaysia's economy grew 4.5% in the second quarter from a year earlier, official advance estimates showed on Friday, sustaining the pace of the previous quarter as strong domestic consumption and steady manufacturing growth offset a slowdown in exports.

In the first quarter, gross domestic product growth grew an annual 4.4%, slowing from the end-2024 rate as lower oil and gas production tempered strong household spending and a steady expansion in investments.

Growth in the April-to-June period was buoyed by resilient consumer demand despite global headwinds, the statistics department said, flagging economic uncertainties driven by U.S. President Donald Trump's tariff drive.

"Overall... the external components of the economy remained challenging, weighed down by surrounding tariff developments and continued global political uncertainties," Chief Statistician Mohd Uzir Mahidin said in a statement.

The services sector remained the primary driver of economic growth in the quarter, growing 5.3% on-year, while the manufacturing sector rose 3.8%, Mohd Uzir said.

Trade activity slowed in May, amid weaker demand in exports, he added.

Separate data released on Friday showed exports falling for the second month in a row in June, declining 3.5% from a year earlier after dipping by 1.1% the previous month.

Malaysia's economy grew 5.1% in 2024, driven by domestic demand, record approved investments, and robust exports.

Prime Minister Anwar Ibrahim said in May that this year's growth target of between 4.5% and 5.5% was unlikely to be met in the aftermath of the U.S. tariffs.

The central bank, which earlier this month cut interest rates for the first time in five years, has also said it would have to lower its growth forecast amid risks to the export-oriented economy from tariffs and geopolitical tensions.

Malaysia faces a 25% tariff on its exports to the United States, unless it can reach a trade deal before August 1.

Final second-quarter GDP figures are expected to be released on August 15.

data.title: Malaysia's June exports record surprise year on year fall of 3.5%
data.description:
Malaysia's exports unexpectedly fell 3.5% in June from a year earlier, defying expectations after a decline in shipments of petroleum products, liquefied natural gas, chemicals, iron and steel and other metals, government data showed on Friday.

June's exports had been expected to grow 5.5% on an annual basis, according to a median forecast among economists surveyed by Reuters.

Imports in June grew 1.2% from a year earlier, data from the trade ministry showed, below the poll forecast of a 10% increase.

Malaysia recorded a trade surplus of 8.6 billion ringgit ($2.03 billion) in June, below the poll's forecast of 10.8 billion ringgit.

($1 = 4.243 ringgit)

data.title: Malaysia's Economy Held Steady Amid Trade Headwinds, Advance Estimates Show
data.description:
(04:00 GMT) *Malaysia 2Q Advance GDP +4.5% on Year; Economists Expected +4.1%
(04:00 GMT) *Malaysia's 1H GDP Estimated +4.4%
(04:00 GMT) Malaysia's Economy Held Steady Amid Trade Headwinds, Advance Estimates Show

By Ying Xian Wong

KUALA LUMPUR--Malaysia's economy fared better than expected in the second quarter despite global uncertainties, advance estimates show.

The Southeast Asian country's gross domestic product grew 4.5% over April-June, supported by robust domestic demand, the Department of Statistics said Friday.

The quarterly print beat a Wall Street Journal poll of economists tipping growth at 4.1%, and was a touch above the 4.4% expansion recorded in the first three months of the year.

That leaves first-half growth at a moderate 4.4%, the department said.

Services, which make up about 58% of Malaysia's GDP, grew 5.3% on year in the second quarter, while the manufacturing sector expanded 3.8%.

Trump administration tariffs continue to hang over Malaysia's economic outlook. Its exports to the U.S. will be subject to a 25% levy if it doesn't secure a trade deal before Aug. 1.

Malaysia's chief statistician noted that the external sector's performance in the second quarter was mixed, with trade activity pulled down significantly in May by weaker demand for goods exports.

While domestic consumption continued to be a strong pillar of support, the external components of the economy remained challenged by tariff developments and global political uncertainty, said Mohd Uzir Mahidin.

Officials in Malaysia are in talks with the U.S. to reach a mutually beneficial agreement, but one has yet to be announced. In Southeast Asia, only Vietnam and Indonesia seem to have struck arrangements to lower tariffs.

If the duty on Malaysia goes into effect, it will weigh on the export-oriented economy, CIMB Securities analyst Ivy Ng Lee Fang and Lim Yue Jia said in a recent note.

They see Malaysia's 2025 GDP growth at 4.3%, down from 5.1% last year, and warn of downside risks if efforts to lower tariffs fail.

That uncertainty factored into the central bank's most recent decision, which saw it cut rates for the first time in five years.

Bank Negara said the move was a pre-emptive measure to cushion the economy against external headwinds.

Final GDP data, due Aug. 15, will confirm the economy's strength during the second quarter.

Write to Ying Xian Wong at yingxian.wong@wsj.com

Italy - Factors to watch on July 18
data.description:
The following factors could affect Italian markets on Friday.

Reuters has not verified the newspaper reports, and cannot vouch for their accuracy. New items are marked with (*).

For a complete list of diary events in Italy please click on .

ECONOMY

Bank of Italy releases May data on foreign investors' holdings of Italian government bonds.

COMPANIES

ENI

The deal to sell half of Eni's carbon capture and storage unit to BlackRock’s Global Infrastructure Partners will likely value the business at around 1 billion euros, Bloomberg News reported on Thursday.

UNICREDIT

A delegation of employee representatives from Commerzbank met with members of the European Parliament to voice their concerns about a possible takeover by the Italian bank, according to a social media post on Thursday and two people with knowledge of the matter.

ILLIMITY

Moody's upgraded on Thursday the long-term deposit and senior unsecured ratings of the digital bank; the outlook is stable.

STELLANTIS

Extraordinary shareholders' meeting to vote on the appointment of Antonio Filosa as new CEO (1100 GMT).

Expected to release Q2 global shipments.

DIARY

Economy Minister Giancarlo Giorgetti attends G20/G7 ministerial meeting in Durban, South Africa.

Rome, trade union CISL 20th congress continues (0900 GMT); ends on July 19. On Friday expected attendees include President of the Italian AI Commission and Pope Francis' AI advisor Paolo Benanti, Labour Minister Marina Calderone, social security agency INAIL President Fabrizio D'Ascenzo, EU Commission Vice President Raffaele Fitto, Vice President Pina Picierno, Foreign Minister Antonio Tajani, European Investment Bank (EIB) Vice President Gelsomina Vigliotti.
